news_summary: Innovative efforts in restoring peatlands, advancing sustainable wind turbine technologies, and raising awareness through powerful Arctic photography highlight significant progress in environmental conservation and climate action.
news_summary_fr: Les initiatives innovantes visant à restaurer les tourbières, à faire progresser les technologies éoliennes durables et à sensibiliser le public grâce à de saisissantes photographies de l'Arctique mettent en évidence les progrès significatifs réalisés en matière de préservation de l'environnement et de lutte contre le changement climatique.
news_summary_es: Las iniciativas innovadoras para la restauración de las turberas, el avance de las tecnologías sostenibles para aerogeneradores y la sensibilización a través de impactantes fotografías del Ártico ponen de relieve los importantes avances en materia de conservación del medio ambiente y lucha contra el cambio climático.

articles.title: Scientist dubbed The Bogfather is restoring peatland to fight climate change
articles.summary: A childhood fascination with bogs led one Welsh scientist to try and help save the planet.
articles.content:
Dr Peter Jones has spent the past 30 years immersed - quite literally - in bogs, fens and wetlands, trying to help save the planet and earning himself the nickname The Bogfather in the process.

These landscapes are now at the forefront of the climate crisis and Jones has been making an offer that policymakers are finding hard to refuse, a nature-based solution that tackles climate change, flooding, wildfires and biodiversity loss all at the same time.

Peatlands store 30% of Wales' land-based carbon, despite covering only 4% of the surface, but they are about 90% degraded, meaning they leak greenhouse gases instead of storing them.

Healthy peatland can slow the flow of water, helping prevent flooding, and act as natural firebreaks during wildfires - both of which are expected to become more common as the climate warms - and protecting them has become Jones's lifelong mission.

"I certainly wouldn't have come up with that myself," he laughs when asked about his nickname.

"A couple of our younger, enthusiastic colleagues started calling me that."

But the name stuck and, given how much work he has done to restore peatland, few would argue with it, said colleague Hanna Huws.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant environmental effort by Dr. Peter Jones to restore peatlands, which provide a nature-based solution addressing climate change, flooding, wildfires, and biodiversity loss. The story has broad significance for climate action and ecosystem health, with clear positive real-world impact and sufficient context about the importance of peatlands.
articles.personality_title: Welsh scientist restores peatlands to fight climate change and protect nature
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Peatlands are wet areas of land that store a lot of carbon, helping to slow down climate change. In Wales, these lands cover only 4% of the surface but hold 30% of the land’s carbon. Unfortunately, about 90% of these peatlands are damaged and release greenhouse gases instead of storing them.

**What happened** – Dr. Peter Jones, a Welsh scientist known as "The Bogfather," has spent 30 years working to restore peatlands. His efforts help the peatlands store carbon again, reduce flooding by slowing water flow, and prevent wildfires by acting as natural firebreaks. His work is gaining attention from policymakers because it addresses climate change, flooding, wildfires, and loss of plants and animals all at once.

**Impact** – Restoring peatlands is important because it helps fight climate change by keeping carbon locked in the ground. It also protects communities from floods and wildfires, which are becoming more common as the Earth warms. Dr. Jones’s work shows how nature can offer solutions to big problems in a way that benefits both people and the environment.

**What's next step** – Policymakers are starting to support peatland restoration projects. This means more funding and efforts could happen to repair damaged peatlands across Wales and possibly other places. Continued work will be needed to keep these lands healthy and to spread the benefits to more areas.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Dr. Peter Jones’s work restoring peatlands offers a natural way to reduce climate change effects and protect communities from floods and wildfires.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un scientifique gallois restaure les tourbières pour lutter contre le changement climatique et protéger la nature
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les tourbières sont des zones humides qui stockent beaucoup de carbone, aidant ainsi à ralentir le changement climatique. Au Pays de Galles, ces terres couvrent seulement 4 % de la surface mais contiennent 30 % du carbone terrestre. Malheureusement, environ 90 % de ces tourbières sont endommagées et libèrent des gaz à effet de serre au lieu de les stocker.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Le Dr Peter Jones, un scientifique gallois surnommé « The Bogfather » (le père des tourbières), travaille depuis 30 ans à restaurer les tourbières. Ses efforts permettent aux tourbières de stocker à nouveau le carbone, de réduire les inondations en ralentissant l'eau et de prévenir les incendies en servant de coupe-feu naturels. Son travail attire l'attention des décideurs car il traite en même temps le changement climatique, les inondations, les incendies et la perte de biodiversité.

**Impact** – Restaurer les tourbières est important car cela aide à lutter contre le changement climatique en gardant le carbone dans le sol. Cela protège aussi les communautés contre les inondations et les incendies, qui deviennent plus fréquents avec le réchauffement de la Terre. Le travail du Dr Jones montre comment la nature peut offrir des solutions aux grands problèmes, bénéfiques pour les humains et l'environnement.

**Prochaine étape** – Les décideurs commencent à soutenir les projets de restauration des tourbières. Cela signifie qu'il pourrait y avoir plus de financements et d'efforts pour réparer les tourbières endommagées au Pays de Galles et ailleurs. Il faudra poursuivre le travail pour garder ces terres en bonne santé et étendre les bénéfices.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le travail du Dr Peter Jones pour restaurer les tourbières offre un moyen naturel de réduire les effets du changement climatique et de protéger les communautés contre les inondations et les incendies.
articles.personality_title_es: Científico galés restaura turberas para combatir el cambio climático y proteger la naturaleza
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las turberas son áreas húmedas que almacenan mucho carbono, ayudando a frenar el cambio climático. En Gales, estas tierras cubren solo el 4 % de la superficie pero contienen el 30 % del carbono terrestre. Desafortunadamente, alrededor del 90 % de estas turberas están dañadas y liberan gases de efecto invernadero en lugar de almacenarlos.

**Qué pasó** – El Dr. Peter Jones, un científico galés apodado "The Bogfather" (El Padre de las Turberas), ha pasado 30 años restaurando turberas. Su trabajo ayuda a que las turberas vuelvan a almacenar carbono, reduce las inundaciones al ralentizar el agua y previene incendios actuando como cortafuegos naturales. Su trabajo está llamando la atención de los responsables políticos porque aborda el cambio climático, las inundaciones, los incendios y la pérdida de biodiversidad al mismo tiempo.

**Impacto** – Restaurar las turberas es importante porque ayuda a combatir el cambio climático al mantener el carbono en el suelo. También protege a las comunidades de inundaciones e incendios, que son más comunes con el calentamiento global. El trabajo del Dr. Jones muestra cómo la naturaleza puede ofrecer soluciones a grandes problemas de manera que benefician a las personas y al medio ambiente.

**Próximo paso** – Los responsables políticos están comenzando a apoyar proyectos de restauración de turberas. Esto podría significar más fondos y esfuerzos para reparar turberas dañadas en Gales y posiblemente en otros lugares. Se necesitará trabajo continuo para mantener estas tierras saludables y expandir los beneficios.

**Frase clave** – El trabajo del Dr. Peter Jones para restaurar turberas ofrece una forma natural de reducir los efectos del cambio climático y proteger a las comunidades de inundaciones e incendios.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a lush, vibrant peatland landscape with deep, rich layers of moss and waterlogged soil gently glowing with captured carbon, interwoven with slow-moving streams that calm floodwaters and natural firebreaks of damp earth, all under a soft golden light symbolizing restoration and hope.

articles.title: Turbines turning from wind to sustainable products
articles.summary: Thousands of tonnes of non-recyclable wind turbine blades are coming to the end of their lives.
articles.content:
When a renewable energy hero needs to be replaced, how do you maintain its green credentials?

That is the challenge facing the wind farm industry, as turbines across the island begin to reach the end of their useful life.

Thousands of tonnes of wind turbine blade material are expected to require replacement by 2030, leading to the question of what to do with them.

Currently, they largely go to landfill or incineration.

The first ever commercial wind farm on the island of Ireland - Bellacorrick in County Mayo - was decommissioned at the start of the year.

Now, two Northern Ireland companies are working together to make the disposal of its blades as eco-friendly as possible.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant environmental challenge—disposal of non-recyclable wind turbine blades—and presents a proactive, eco-friendly solution by companies collaborating to recycle or repurpose these materials. This addresses a growing issue in renewable energy sustainability with broad environmental impact and meaningful real-world benefits.
articles.personality_title: Turning Old Wind Turbine Blades into Sustainable Materials
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Wind turbines help produce clean energy, but their large blades are hard to recycle when they get old. Many blades now end up in landfills or are burned, which can harm the environment.

**What happened** – The first wind farm in Ireland, Bellacorrick, was recently shut down. Two companies in Northern Ireland are now working together to find eco-friendly ways to recycle or reuse the old turbine blades instead of throwing them away.

**Impact** – This effort is important because by 2030, thousands of tonnes of these blades will need replacing. Finding new uses for them stops waste from piling up and keeps the renewable energy process more environmentally friendly.

**What's next step** – The companies plan to develop new methods to recycle or transform the blades into useful products. This could set an example for other wind farms around the world facing the same challenge.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Two Northern Ireland companies are creating eco-friendly solutions to recycle old wind turbine blades, reducing waste and supporting clean energy sustainability.
articles.personality_title_fr: Transformer les vieilles pales d’éoliennes en matériaux durables
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les éoliennes produisent de l’énergie propre, mais leurs grandes pales sont difficiles à recycler une fois usées. Beaucoup finissent dans des décharges ou sont brûlées, ce qui peut nuire à l’environnement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – La première ferme éolienne d’Irlande, Bellacorrick, a récemment été fermée. Deux entreprises d’Irlande du Nord travaillent maintenant ensemble pour recycler ou réutiliser ces vieilles pales de manière écologique plutôt que de les jeter.

**Impact** – Cette initiative est importante car d’ici 2030, des milliers de tonnes de pales devront être remplacées. Trouver de nouvelles utilisations évite l’accumulation de déchets et rend l’énergie renouvelable plus respectueuse de l’environnement.

**Prochaine étape** – Les entreprises prévoient de développer de nouvelles façons de recycler ou transformer les pales en produits utiles. Cela pourrait servir d’exemple aux autres fermes éoliennes dans le monde.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Deux entreprises d’Irlande du Nord créent des solutions écologiques pour recycler les vieilles pales d’éoliennes, réduisant les déchets et soutenant la durabilité énergétique.
articles.personality_title_es: Convertir las viejas palas de aerogeneradores en materiales sostenibles
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los aerogeneradores producen energía limpia, pero sus grandes palas son difíciles de reciclar cuando se vuelven viejas. Muchas terminan en vertederos o se queman, lo que puede dañar el medio ambiente.

**Qué pasó** – El primer parque eólico de Irlanda, Bellacorrick, se cerró recientemente. Dos empresas de Irlanda del Norte están trabajando juntas para reciclar o reutilizar las palas viejas de manera ecológica en lugar de tirarlas.

**Impacto** – Este esfuerzo es importante porque para 2030 se necesitará reemplazar miles de toneladas de estas palas. Encontrar nuevos usos evita la acumulación de residuos y mantiene la energía renovable más amigable con el ambiente.

**Próximo paso** – Las empresas planean desarrollar métodos para reciclar o transformar las palas en productos útiles. Esto podría ser un ejemplo para otros parques eólicos con el mismo problema.

**Resumen en una frase** – Dos empresas de Irlanda del Norte crean soluciones ecológicas para reciclar viejas palas de aerogeneradores, reduciendo residuos y apoyando la sostenibilidad de la energía limpia.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of large wind turbine blades gently transforming into smooth, eco-friendly products like wooden furniture and recycled materials, set against a soft, natural landscape of rolling Irish hills under a calm sky, with silhouettes of two collaborating figures represented by intertwined hands made of leaves, symbolizing sustainable renewal and partnership.

articles.title: Evocative photos of Canadian Arctic win New Scientist Editors Award
articles.summary: Natalya Saprunova's photo series exploring coastal erosion and permafrost thaw across Inuvialuit territories in Canada has won the New Scientist Editors Award at the Earth Photo 2026 competition
articles.content:
Natalya Saprunova's photo series exploring coastal erosion and permafrost thaw across Inuvialuit territories in Canada has won the New Scientist Editors Award at the Earth Photo 2026 competition

A hunter in Tuktoyaktuk in Canada’s Northwest Territories uses decoy geese to lure birds Natalya Saprunova

The realities of a changing global climate collide with years of tradition in Natalya Saprunova’s icy blue photograph, above, part of a series that won the New Scientist Editors Award in the Earth Photo 2026 competition.

The image shows a hunter from an Inuit community in Tuktoyaktuk on Canada’s Arctic coast holding a goose decoy meant to lure migrating birds. In the background, a pale sky touches banks of melting ice and murky water already studded with several faux birds. Indigenous peoples in the region used to make these decoys from reeds, but it isn’t just the materials they work with that have now changed – rising temperatures have affected the actual birds, shifting their migratory patterns and making them harder to hunt. Saprunova documents this and other related changes in her winning series, focusing especially on the melting permafrost.

Below, an Inuit resident of Victoria Island handles fish, another essential resource for the local community, and another animal whose behaviour has changed with the changing climate. Because permafrost thawing accelerates coastal erosion, it also introduces harmful compounds like mercury into the habitats of commonly eaten fish, endangering food supplies.

In Ulukhaktok, on Victoria Island, a resident deals with fish – a vital food source for the community Natalya Saprunova

Taking a wider view in the image below, Saprunova captures the texture of changes to the Arctic landscape itself, a web of sunken polygons filled with water and occasionally studded with conical, ice-cored hills. As permafrost melts, the land becomes uneven and makes it harder for animals like caribou to traverse their home. “The thaw is not just melting ice, it is reshaping the map upon which animals and people have always relied,” writes Saprunova in her submission for the prize.

Near Tuktoyaktuk, the permafrost thaw is transforming the landscape Natalya Saprunova

The situation is even more dire when she photographs the hamlet of Sachs Harbour where whole cliffs of permafrost are disappearing. A rugged, uneven cliff laced with cracks is shown dangerously close to homes, below. The contrast between these neat residences and the eroding land conveys the urgency of climate catastrophe in the Arctic. Canada has the longest inhabited Arctic coastline in the world and some of its inhabitants stand the grim chance of becoming the country’s first climate refugees.

An eroding permafrost cliff in Sachs Harbour, Banks Island Natalya Saprunova

Accordingly, Saprunova photographs Pelly Island, below, which is known to be disappearing. The permafrost that once comprised it is now melting away and releasing greenhouse gases into the atmosphere, which could, devastatingly, speed up both the rise in global temperatures and the further melting of the island. A cliff of black rock looks monstrously barren, as a nearby tiny human figure looks at the water. Exposed veins of gray and white rock only underline how much climate change is wounding their world.

Pelly Island, also known as the disappearing island in north-western Canada Natalya Saprunova

Images from this series will be shown at an exhibition at the Royal Geographical Society in London until 24 July.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights an award-winning photo series that documents the tangible impacts of climate change on the Canadian Arctic, including permafrost thaw, coastal erosion, and effects on indigenous communities and wildlife. This raises awareness about urgent environmental issues with broad significance for global climate action and indigenous rights, supported by detailed context and vivid imagery.
articles.personality_title: Photo series reveals climate change effects in Canadian Arctic
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Canadian Arctic is experiencing major changes because of climate change. Melting ice and thawing permafrost are affecting the land, animals, and the people who live there, especially Indigenous communities.

**What happened** – Photographer Natalya Saprunova created a series of photos showing these changes in the Inuvialuit territories of Canada’s Arctic coast. Her work won the New Scientist Editors Award at the Earth Photo 2026 competition. The photos show hunters using traditional decoys, melting ice, eroding cliffs near homes, and disappearing islands.

**Impact** – Saprunova’s photos make visible the real effects of climate change on both nature and culture. For example, melting permafrost is causing the land to sink and crack, making it harder for animals like caribou to move. It also releases harmful substances into water, which risks fish that local people depend on for food. Her images highlight how climate change threatens not just the environment but also the way of life for Indigenous communities.

**What's next step** – These photos are displayed at the Royal Geographical Society in London until July 24. They can help raise awareness about the urgent need to protect Arctic environments and support Indigenous peoples facing these challenges. The images may inspire more research and action on climate change effects in the north.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Natalya Saprunova’s award-winning photos clearly show how climate change is reshaping the Canadian Arctic’s land, wildlife, and Indigenous lives.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une série photo dévoile les effets du changement climatique dans l’Arctique canadien
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’Arctique canadien subit d’importants changements dus au changement climatique. La fonte des glaces et le dégel du pergélisol affectent la terre, les animaux et les populations locales, notamment les communautés autochtones.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – La photographe Natalya Saprunova a réalisé une série de photos montrant ces changements dans les territoires inuvialuits sur la côte arctique du Canada. Son travail a remporté le New Scientist Editors Award au concours Earth Photo 2026. Les photos montrent des chasseurs utilisant des leurres traditionnels, la fonte des glaces, des falaises érodées près des habitations et des îles qui disparaissent.

**Impact** – Les photos de Saprunova rendent visibles les effets concrets du changement climatique sur la nature et la culture. Par exemple, le dégel du pergélisol fait s’affaisser et se fissurer la terre, ce qui complique les déplacements des animaux comme le caribou. Il libère aussi des substances nocives dans l’eau, menaçant les poissons dont dépendent les habitants. Ses images soulignent que le changement climatique menace non seulement l’environnement, mais aussi le mode de vie des communautés autochtones.

**Prochaines étapes** – Ces photos sont exposées à la Royal Geographical Society de Londres jusqu’au 24 juillet. Elles peuvent aider à sensibiliser sur la nécessité urgente de protéger les environnements arctiques et de soutenir les peuples autochtones face à ces défis. Les images pourraient encourager plus de recherches et d’actions sur les effets du changement climatique dans le Nord.

**En une phrase** – Les photos primées de Natalya Saprunova montrent clairement comment le changement climatique transforme la terre, la faune et la vie des peuples autochtones dans l’Arctique canadien.
articles.personality_title_es: Serie fotográfica muestra los efectos del cambio climático en el Ártico canadiense
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Ártico canadiense está experimentando grandes cambios debido al cambio climático. El hielo que se derrite y el deshielo del permafrost están afectando la tierra, los animales y las personas que viven allí, especialmente las comunidades indígenas.

**Qué pasó** – La fotógrafa Natalya Saprunova creó una serie de fotos que muestran estos cambios en los territorios Inuvialuit de la costa ártica de Canadá. Su trabajo ganó el New Scientist Editors Award en la competencia Earth Photo 2026. Las fotos muestran cazadores usando señuelos tradicionales, hielo derritiéndose, acantilados que se erosionan cerca de casas y islas que desaparecen.

**Impacto** – Las fotos de Saprunova hacen visibles los efectos reales del cambio climático en la naturaleza y la cultura. Por ejemplo, el deshielo del permafrost hace que la tierra se hunda y se agriete, dificultando el paso de animales como el caribú. También libera sustancias dañinas en el agua, poniendo en riesgo a los peces de los que dependen las personas locales para alimentarse. Sus imágenes muestran que el cambio climático amenaza no solo el medio ambiente sino también la forma de vida de las comunidades indígenas.

**Próximo paso** – Estas fotos se exhiben en la Royal Geographical Society en Londres hasta el 24 de julio. Pueden ayudar a crear conciencia sobre la necesidad urgente de proteger los ambientes árticos y apoyar a los pueblos indígenas que enfrentan estos desafíos. Las imágenes podrían inspirar más investigaciones y acciones sobre los efectos del cambio climático en el norte.

**Una frase clave** – Las fotos premiadas de Natalya Saprunova muestran claramente cómo el cambio climático está transformando la tierra, la vida silvestre y las vidas indígenas en el Ártico canadiense.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a serene Arctic coastal landscape featuring melting permafrost cliffs gently eroding into calm waters dotted with delicate, handcrafted goose decoys floating like small boats, alongside abstract polygonal patterns in the thawing ground symbolizing shifting terrain, all rendered in soft, natural blues, grays, and earth tones with warm golden light suggesting hope and resilience.
